programs.name: Medical Assistance (MA) for Seniors
programs.tagline: Comprehensive health coverage including Medicare premium help and home care services for Minnesota seniors 65+.
programs.intro: If your parent is 65+ and struggling with medical costs, Minnesota Medical Assistance can cover doctor visits, hospital care, prescription drugs, and long-term services like home care or assisted living support. Because MA works alongside Medicare for eligible seniors, your parent could get help paying Medicare premiums plus coverage for services Medicare doesn't cover. Income limits vary: up to $1,305/month for disability-based MA or higher limits (up to 138% of federal poverty level) for general coverage. Some seniors qualify automatically based on other benefits they receive, while others must meet income and asset tests.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: For waiver services like the Elderly Waiver: must need nursing facility level of care (daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or similar needs) but want to stay in the community
otherRequirements: May need Medicare Parts A & B enrollment
structuredEligibility.povertyLevelReference: 100% FPL for disability-based; 138% FPL for general coverage

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at MNsure.org or call 1-855-366-7873 — processing time varies by county.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of income (Social Security award letters), bank statements, Medicare cards, and ID documents

steps.title: Apply through MNsure or county office
steps.description: Complete the Medical Assistance application online at MNsure.org, by phone at 1-855-366-7873, or at your local county human services office

steps.title: Request waiver assessment if needed
steps.description: If your parent needs home care or assisted living support, ask for a MnCHOICES assessment to determine eligibility for waiver services like the Elderly Waiver

applicationGuide.waitlist: Waiver services like the Elderly Waiver may have waitlists due to funding caps
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent has Medicare, mention this during application — they may qualify for special dual-eligible plans that coordinate both programs.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size (Disability-Based MA)
contentSections.footnote: Based on 100% Federal Poverty Guidelines; general MA allows higher incomes up to 138% FPL

tiers.name: Standard MA
tiers.description: Basic health coverage for seniors who qualify
tiers.coverage: Doctor visits, hospital care, prescription drugs, no monthly premium

tiers.name: Medicare Savings Programs
tiers.description: For seniors with both Medicare and MA
tiers.coverage: Helps pay Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copays

tiers.name: Elderly Waiver Services
tiers.description: For seniors needing nursing home level care
tiers.coverage: Home care, adult day care, assisted living support, case management

contentSections.text: Waiver services like home care and assisted living support have waitlists due to funding limits. Apply early if your parent needs these services.
Not all seniors 65+ qualify automatically. Most must meet income and asset tests, but some qualify based on receiving other benefits like SSI.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for Medical Assistance?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home is typically exempt from asset limits, and there's no lien placed on the house if your parent is eligible for MA.

faqs.question: What if Dad already has Medicare — can he still get Medical Assistance?
faqs.answer: Yes. If he qualifies, MA can help pay Medicare premiums and cover services Medicare doesn't, like long-term care. He may be eligible for special dual-eligible plans like MSHO or MSC+.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist for home care services?
faqs.answer: Waitlist length varies by county and funding availability. The Elderly Waiver, which covers home care and assisted living support, has caps that can create waitlists. Apply early and ask your county office for current wait times.

faqs.question: What's the difference between regular Medical Assistance and the Elderly Waiver?
faqs.answer: Regular MA covers medical care like doctor visits and hospital stays. The Elderly Waiver adds home care, adult day care, and assisted living support for seniors who need nursing home level care but want to stay in the community.

faqs.question: Can a married couple protect assets if one spouse needs Medical Assistance?
faqs.answer: Yes. Spousal impoverishment rules protect some assets and income for the spouse who doesn't need MA. The exact amounts depend on your situation — discuss this during the application process.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: She may still qualify through different pathways. MA has multiple eligibility categories with different income limits, and some allow higher incomes up to 138% of federal poverty level. Apply to find out which pathway works.

programs.name: Elderly Waiver (EW) and Alternative Care (AC) Program
programs.tagline: Get home care services paid for so your parent can stay out of a nursing home.

localEntities.name: County or tribal Long-Term Care Consultation offices

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management, Minnesota will pay for home care services to keep them out of a nursing home. The Elderly Waiver covers services like personal care, home health aides, meal delivery, and emergency response systems for parents who qualify for Medical Assistance. Alternative Care provides similar services for parents whose income is too high for Medical Assistance but still can't afford nursing home care for more than 135 days.

Both programs require your parent to need nursing home-level care but choose to stay in the community instead. Services include adult day care, housekeeping, transportation, respite care for family caregivers, and specialized medical equipment. Because these programs cap service costs at nursing home rates, your parent gets comprehensive care coordination without the expense of institutional care.

exemptAssets: Primary home (equity up to $730,000 if intent to return)

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need nursing home-level care (daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, mobility, or medication management)
otherRequirements: Service costs must be less than nursing home placement

applicationGuide.summary: Call your county's Long-Term Care office to schedule a MnCHOICES assessment — processing time not specified.

steps.title: Contact your county office
steps.description: Call your county or tribal Long-Term Care Consultation office to request a MnCHOICES assessment. You can also call Minnesota Aging Pathways at 800-333-2433 for referral.

steps.title: Complete MnCHOICES assessment
steps.description: A county assessor will evaluate your parent's daily living needs to determine if they meet nursing home-level care requirements.

steps.title: Financial eligibility review
steps.description: County will review income and assets to determine if your parent qualifies for Elderly Waiver (needs Medical Assistance) or Alternative Care (pays monthly fee).

steps.title: Service plan development
steps.description: If approved, case manager creates service plan within budget caps and arranges providers for approved services.

applicationGuide.tip: Gather financial documents before calling — the county will need bank statements, income records, and home equity information for the eligibility determination.

tiers.name: Elderly Waiver (EW)
tiers.description: For parents who qualify for Medical Assistance
tiers.incomeLimit: Must meet Medical Assistance income limits
tiers.coverage: Fully funded services. No monthly fee.

tiers.name: Alternative Care (AC)
tiers.description: For parents whose income is too high for Medical Assistance
tiers.incomeLimit: Income/assets inadequate to pay for nursing home beyond 135 days
tiers.coverage: Cost-sharing program. Pay monthly assessed fee. Service costs must be under 75% of average nursing home payment.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Primary home (if equity under $730,000 and intent to return)
Burial funds up to certain limits
Income-producing property if essential to support

categories.name: Financial
items: Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Home deed and recent mortgage statement

contentSections.text: 60-month look-back rule applies — any assets transferred for less than fair market value in the past 5 years may result in a penalty period where your parent is ineligible for services.
Your parent must choose community services over nursing home placement. If they want nursing home care instead, these programs don't apply.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies?
faqs.answer: Yes, if she intends to return home or if a spouse, minor child, or disabled adult child lives there. Home equity must be under $730,000 (2025 limit). The house is an exempt asset and won't count toward the $2,000 asset limit.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over the Medical Assistance limit?
faqs.answer: He may qualify for Alternative Care instead of Elderly Waiver. Alternative Care serves people whose income is too high for Medical Assistance but still can't afford nursing home care for more than 135 days. He'll pay a monthly assessed fee for services.

faqs.question: Does dementia automatically qualify my parent?
faqs.answer: No. Your parent must need nursing home-level care — daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, eating, mobility, or medication management. Dementia alone isn't sufficient unless it affects their ability to perform these daily living activities.

faqs.question: What happens if the services cost more than a nursing home?
faqs.answer: Services won't be approved. For Elderly Waiver, service costs must be less than nursing home placement. For Alternative Care, costs must be under 75% of the average Medical Assistance nursing home payment rate.

faqs.question: Can I get respite care as the family caregiver?
faqs.answer: Yes, respite care is a covered service under both programs. The program can also provide training for family caregivers to help you better care for your parent at home.

faqs.question: What if we gave money to grandchildren in the past few years?
faqs.answer: Any gifts or asset transfers in the past 60 months (5 years) will be reviewed. Transfers for less than fair market value may result in a penalty period where your parent is temporarily ineligible for services.

programs.name: Minnesota Senior Health Options (MSHO)
programs.tagline: Combines your parent's Medicare and Medicaid into one plan with coordinated care, including home care and nursing facility services.

localEntities.name: Administered by MN DHS and seven health plans; check county-specific plans via DHS.

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and receives Medicaid (called Medical Assistance in Minnesota), MSHO can simplify their healthcare by combining Medicare and Medicaid into one managed care plan. Instead of coordinating between separate providers, your parent gets one care coordinator who manages everything — doctors, hospital care, home care, and nursing facility services.

MSHO covers all the standard Medicare and Medicaid services, plus additional benefits like personal care assistance, transportation, and up to 180 days of nursing home care if your parent enters from the community. Because it's a managed care plan, your parent pays $0 for most services including wellness visits, specialist care, and cancer screenings.

The program is only available in certain Minnesota counties and requires your parent to already qualify for Medical Assistance (Medicaid). If they have Medicare, they must have both Part A and Part B.

summary: Must already qualify for Medical Assistance (Medicaid)
Medicare Parts A and B required if Medicare-eligible
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Some services like personal care assistance require demonstrated need with at least one Activity of Daily Living (bathing, dressing, eating, transferring, toileting, or continence)
otherRequirements: Must be enrolled in Medical Assistance (Medicaid)
Must live in a county where MSHO is offered
If Medicare-eligible, must have both Part A and Part B

applicationGuide.summary: Call Minnesota's Health Care Consumer Support for choice counseling — they'll help you pick the right MSHO health plan in your parent's county.

steps.title: Confirm Medical Assistance eligibility
steps.description: Your parent must already be enrolled in Medical Assistance (Medicaid). If not enrolled, apply for that first.

steps.title: Check county availability
steps.description: Verify that MSHO is offered in your parent's county and which health plans are available there.

steps.title: Call for choice counseling
steps.description: Contact Health Care Consumer Support at 1-800-657-3672 to discuss which MSHO plan works best for your parent's needs.

steps.title: Enroll with chosen health plan
steps.description: Complete enrollment directly with the selected health plan (like UCare, Medica, or Blue Cross SecureBlue).

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent already lives in a nursing facility, MSHO won't cover those costs — the program only pays for nursing home care if your parent enters from the community.

categories.name: Age and Residency
items: Proof of Minnesota residency in a county where MSHO is offered

categories.name: Insurance
items: Medicare Part A and Part B enrollment cards (if Medicare-eligible)

contentSections.text: MSHO only covers nursing facility services for the first 180 days if your parent enters from the community. If they're already living in a nursing home when they enroll, those costs won't be covered.

faqs.question: What if my parent doesn't qualify for Medical Assistance yet?
faqs.answer: Your parent must be enrolled in Medical Assistance (Minnesota's Medicaid program) before they can join MSHO. Apply for Medical Assistance first through your county or online at mn.gov.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their current doctors with MSHO?
faqs.answer: It depends on the MSHO health plan you choose and whether your parent's doctors are in that plan's network. Each health plan (UCare, Medica, Blue Cross, etc.) has different provider networks, so ask about your parent's current doctors when comparing plans.

faqs.question: What happens if MSHO isn't available in my parent's county?
faqs.answer: If your parent is 65+ and doesn't have access to MSHO, they may be automatically enrolled in Minnesota Senior Care Plus (MSC+), another program that coordinates Medicare and Medicaid services.

faqs.question: Will my parent get help coordinating all their care?
faqs.answer: Yes. MSHO assigns your parent a care coordinator who helps access services and coordinates between different providers. Because everything is under one plan, there's less paperwork and fewer phone calls for you to manage.

faqs.question: What if my parent needs nursing home care later?
faqs.answer: MSHO covers up to 180 days of nursing facility services, but only if your parent enters from the community (not from a hospital). If they're already in a nursing home when they enroll in MSHO, those costs won't be covered by the program.

faqs.question: Is enrollment in MSHO required?
faqs.answer: No, MSHO enrollment is voluntary. However, if your parent is 65+ with both Medicare and Medicaid and doesn't choose MSHO, they may be automatically enrolled in an alternative program like Minnesota Senior Care Plus.
